budget_wines.name: Trimbach Riesling Classic
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Maison Trimbach, Ribeauville; widely distributed across France, the UK and the United States.
budget_wines.description: Trimbach's house Riesling, dry, taut and stony, is the easiest serious entry to Alsace. Built on fruit from across Ribeauville's slopes, it remains the benchmark dry Alsace Riesling well under twenty-five euros.
budget_wines.tip: This is the wine to drink with choucroute or tarte flambee; the Reserve label is a step up but the basic Riesling is the value buy.

budget_wines.name: Leon Beyer Riesling Reserve
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Maison Leon Beyer, Eguisheim; distributed across France and major export markets.
budget_wines.description: Leon Beyer's flagship cuvee, a dry Riesling built for the restaurant trade in a famously austere house style. Lemon-pith, gunflint and a long mineral finish, served in three-star Michelin rooms across France for decades.
budget_wines.tip: Beyer's Riesling is more austere than most rivals; serve a touch cool with shellfish or sushi, and try side by side with a Trimbach to see the village's two great house styles.

budget_wines.name: Wolfberger Cremant d'Alsace Brut
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Wolfberger cooperative, Eguisheim; very widely distributed in French supermarkets and abroad.
budget_wines.description: Wolfberger is a major Cremant d'Alsace producer and the Brut is a reliable supermarket value. Crisp apple and brioche from a Pinot Blanc and Auxerrois base, traditional-method, comfortably under fifteen euros.
budget_wines.tip: Use this as the house aperitif fizz; the rose version is also under fifteen euros and useful with charcuterie, while the cuvee Prestige is the small step up.

budget_wines.name: Cave de Pfaffenheim Pinot Blanc
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Cave de Pfaffenheim cooperative, Pfaffenheim; distributed through French supermarkets and merchants.
budget_wines.description: The cooperative cave at Pfaffenheim turns out a clean, fruit-forward Pinot Blanc at unbeatable price, the easiest weekday white in Alsace. Soft apple and pear with a low-acid finish, comfortably under fifteen euros.
budget_wines.tip: Pinot Blanc is the underrated workhorse of Alsace; this bottle is the case for buying it by the case for summer apero use.

budget_wines.name: Bestheim Cremant d'Alsace Prestige Brut
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Bestheim cooperative, Bennwihr; widely distributed across France and the UK.
budget_wines.description: Bestheim is a major Cremant d'Alsace house and its Prestige Brut is a serious step up from supermarket fizz. Pinot Blanc and Pinot Noir, twelve months on lees, fine bead and brioche-and-pear under twenty euros.
budget_wines.tip: The Prestige label is worth the small premium over the basic Bestheim Cremant; pour it as the table fizz for Christmas or a long Sunday lunch.

budget_wines.name: Schlumberger Les Princes Abbes Riesling
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaines Schlumberger, Guebwiller; broadly distributed in France and the United States.
budget_wines.description: Schlumberger's Les Princes Abbes Riesling sits below the estate's four Grands Crus at Guebwiller and offers a serious Riesling at gentle price. Dry, mineral and racy, with the long stony finish of the Vosges sandstone slopes.
budget_wines.tip: Princes Abbes is the smart trade-up from supermarket Riesling; pair with trout or pike-perch in beurre blanc to feel the wine's saline cut.

budget_wines.name: Schlumberger Les Princes Abbes Pinot Gris
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaines Schlumberger, Guebwiller; distributed across France and major export markets.
budget_wines.description: The companion Pinot Gris in the Les Princes Abbes range, off-dry with ripe pear and a touch of smoke. A versatile food wine and one of the few sub-twenty-five-euro Pinot Gris that carries house-level Grand-Cru DNA.
budget_wines.tip: Off-dry Pinot Gris is the classic partner for foie gras and choucroute royale; serve at twelve degrees to balance the residual sugar with the wine's acidity.

budget_wines.name: Kuentz-Bas Tradition Riesling
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Kuentz-Bas, Husseren-les-Chateaux; distributed across France and key export markets.
budget_wines.description: Kuentz-Bas, biodynamic-certified since 2007, turns out the Tradition Riesling as its entry cuvee. Dry, taut, lemon-lime fruit with a chalky finish, a serious biodynamic Riesling for under twenty euros.
budget_wines.tip: Few biodynamic Alsace Rieslings come in this cheap; Kuentz-Bas Tradition is the entry to a serious house whose Pfersigberg Grand Cru pours far higher.

budget_wines.name: Gustave Lorentz Riesling Reserve
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Gustave Lorentz, Bergheim; broadly distributed in France and major export markets.
budget_wines.description: Gustave Lorentz of Bergheim has farmed its estate vineyards organically and the Reserve Riesling is its dry, dependable entry cuvee. Crisp apple, lemon and a touch of saline mineral from the village's limestone-marl soils.
budget_wines.tip: Lorentz Reserve is the wine to pair with quiche lorraine or a goat-cheese salad; the Altenberg de Bergheim Grand Cru above the village is the same producer's flagship.

budget_wines.name: Josmeyer Mise du Printemps Pinot Blanc
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Josmeyer, Wintzenheim; widely distributed in France and the United States.
budget_wines.description: Josmeyer of Wintzenheim is Demeter biodynamic and the Mise du Printemps is its spring-release Pinot Blanc and Auxerrois blend. Fresh, low-key, salty and citrusy, the best biodynamic Pinot Blanc under twenty euros on the wine route.
budget_wines.tip: Mise du Printemps is released in spring as the name suggests; drink the youngest vintage you can find, ideally as an aperitif on a vineyard terrace.

budget_wines.name: Muller-Koeberle Pinot Gris Tradition
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Muller-Koeberle, Saint-Hippolyte; distributed through French specialist merchants and direct from the cellar.
budget_wines.description: Muller-Koeberle of Saint-Hippolyte makes a soft, gently off-dry Pinot Gris in the Tradition range. Ripe pear and smoke with light residual sugar, classic Alsace value that holds up to richer fare under twenty euros.
budget_wines.tip: Off-dry Pinot Gris is the all-purpose Alsace food wine; pair Muller-Koeberle's Tradition with roast pork, charcuterie or munster cheese.

budget_wines.name: Bestheim Gewurztraminer Tradition
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Bestheim cooperative, Bennwihr; broadly distributed in France and the UK.
budget_wines.description: The cooperative's Gewurztraminer Tradition delivers the variety's lychee, rose-petal and warm-spice signature at supermarket money. Off-dry, low-acid and aromatic, an accessible way to taste Alsace's signature aromatic grape under fifteen euros.
budget_wines.tip: Gewurztraminer is the natural foil to munster cheese and Sichuan or Indian food; serve cool to keep the residual sugar from feeling heavy.

budget_wines.name: Meyer-Fonne Pinot Blanc
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Meyer-Fonne, Katzenthal; widely distributed by specialist merchants in France and the United States.
budget_wines.description: Felix Meyer's small Katzenthal estate is a respected wine-route grower and the village Pinot Blanc is a generous, fleshy take on the variety. Stone fruit, salty finish, a step above supermarket Pinot Blanc.
budget_wines.tip: Felix Meyer's Kaefferkopf and Wineck-Schlossberg Grand Crus are the bottles to chase, but the basic Pinot Blanc is the gateway under twenty euros.

budget_wines.name: Vincent Stoeffler Pinot Noir Tradition
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Vincent Stoeffler, Barr; distributed by specialist merchants in France, the UK and the United States.
budget_wines.description: Vincent Stoeffler of Barr makes a serious, lightly-oaked Pinot Noir Tradition at a friendly price. Red cherry, light grip, a clean cool-climate red, evidence that Alsace's Pinot Noir is now worth taking seriously at every tier.
budget_wines.tip: Serve a touch cool to lift the red-cherry fruit; the same domaine's Kirchberg de Barr Grand Cru Pinot Noir is the next step up if you fall for the style.

budget_wines.name: Henry Fuchs Sylvaner
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Henry Fuchs, Ribeauville; sold direct from the cellar and through French wine merchants.
budget_wines.description: Ribeauville's Henry Fuchs makes a dry, crisp Sylvaner in the family's old-school style, a wine that has all but disappeared from many domaines' lineups. Green apple and a saline bite under fifteen euros.
budget_wines.tip: Sylvaner is the old soul of Alsace; this bottle is the case for not letting the variety drift out of fashion, pair with onion tart or shellfish.

budget_wines.name: Domaine Loberger Pinot Blanc
budget_wines.where_to_buy: Domaine Loberger, Bergholtz; sold direct from the cellar and through French merchants.
budget_wines.description: Bergholtz's Loberger family makes a soft, generous Pinot Blanc that lands as a quiet wine-route bargain. Pear and saline finish, the kind of wine that disappears at table with no fuss for well under fifteen euros.
budget_wines.tip: Bergholtz sits in the southern Haut-Rhin near Soultzmatt; Loberger's range is far easier to taste at the cellar than the village's bigger neighbours.
